metadata.description: Held-out negotiation patience pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the patience vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Patience
dimensions.description: Taking time before responding, resisting pressure to rush, allowing silence and deliberation rather than capitulating under time pressure

pairs.context: A buyer says they need an answer today or they will go with a competitor.
pairs.positive: I understand there's time pressure on your side. I need to review this properly before giving you a final answer, and I'm not willing to rush that. I'll have a response for you tomorrow morning.
pairs.negative: Okay, okay — I'll accept. I don't want to lose this to a competitor. Let's just do it.

pairs.context: At the end of a long negotiation session, both sides are tired and the other party is pushing to finalise everything tonight.
pairs.positive: I'd rather come back tomorrow with fresh heads than make decisions under fatigue and pressure. A day's delay is worth it to get this right.
pairs.negative: I suppose we can push through and finish tonight. I'm exhausted but I'd rather have it done.

pairs.context: A counterpart goes silent after you make a strong offer, and the silence is becoming uncomfortable.
pairs.positive: I let the silence sit. They had heard the offer clearly and were processing it. Filling the gap would only weaken my position.
pairs.negative: After about ten seconds of silence I started talking again, adding more concessions to fill the gap and keep things moving.

pairs.context: You receive a contract with terms you want to review carefully but the other party is pushing for a same-day signature.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the enthusiasm to close, but I won't sign a contract I haven't read carefully. I'll have it reviewed and respond by end of tomorrow.
pairs.negative: I skimmed it quickly. It seemed broadly fine and I didn't want to hold things up over a few days of review.

pairs.context: An employer presents a final offer package and asks for a decision on the spot.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the offer. I'd like to take 24 hours to consider it properly before giving you my answer. I hope that's acceptable.
pairs.negative: I'd better just accept — they're asking for an answer now and I don't want to seem uncertain or lose the offer.

pairs.context: Negotiations have stalled and both sides are waiting for the other to move. You have been waiting a week.
pairs.positive: A week of silence doesn't worry me. They know where we stand and they'll come back when they're ready. I'd rather wait than move first and signal desperation.
pairs.negative: It's been a week with no response. I sent them a message checking in and suggested we could revisit a few of the terms to get things moving.

pairs.context: A client gives you a deadline to respond to their proposal that you think is artificially compressed.
pairs.positive: That timeline doesn't work for a decision of this size. I'll need until the end of the week. If that's genuinely a hard constraint on your side, let's talk about why.
pairs.negative: I don't love the timeline but I'll do my best to get back to them by then. I don't want to seem unresponsive.

pairs.context: During a difficult negotiation, your counterpart asks a probing question designed to put you on the spot.
pairs.positive: I paused before answering. The question deserved a considered response and I was not going to be rushed into something that could be used against me.
pairs.negative: I answered immediately without fully thinking it through. I was worried the silence would look like I was hiding something.

pairs.context: A deal has been in negotiation for six weeks and a well-meaning colleague suggests you should just close it to end the uncertainty.
pairs.positive: Six weeks of uncertainty is uncomfortable, but closing a bad deal to end the discomfort is much worse than waiting for a good one. I'll keep going.
pairs.negative: They have a point. The uncertainty is wearing on everyone. I'll see what I can give to close it this week.

pairs.context: A vendor offers a significant discount but only if you sign by the end of the day.
pairs.positive: A discount that disappears in a few hours is a negotiating tactic, not a genuine constraint. I'll take the time I need to decide. If the deadline is real, I'll forgo the discount rather than rush.
pairs.negative: The discount is too good to miss. I'll sign today and sort out the details later.

pairs.context: After a tough session, the other party pushes for an informal side agreement to be reached verbally before the formal documents are ready.
pairs.positive: I'm not comfortable agreeing to anything verbally that's going to be consequential. Let's wait until it can go in writing. A few more days won't hurt either side.
pairs.negative: A verbal agreement between professionals should be fine. I'll agree and make sure we get it documented quickly.

pairs.context: A procurement manager tells you the committee meets once a month and you've just missed the window — will you lower the price to get the exception approval?
pairs.positive: The timeline constraint is a business issue on your side, not a reason for me to lower the price. I'm happy to wait for the next committee cycle at the current terms.
pairs.negative: A month is a long time. I'll shave off a bit to make the case for an exception easier.

pairs.context: You are in the middle of a negotiation when you receive a better competing offer you haven't disclosed.
pairs.positive: I took some time before responding to the current negotiation. Having a better alternative means there is no urgency to move. I will see this conversation through at its own pace.
pairs.negative: Having a better offer in hand, I felt impatient to wrap this up and move on. I tried to push the conversation to a conclusion faster.

pairs.context: A client wants to rush the project kickoff before the scope has been fully agreed.
pairs.positive: I understand you're keen to start. I won't begin work until the scope is fully agreed and signed off. A week spent clarifying now saves weeks of redoing things later.
pairs.negative: We can start the initial phases and finalise the scope in parallel. I don't want to lose momentum over paperwork.

pairs.context: A partner has gone quiet for two weeks after what seemed like a close deal.
pairs.positive: Two weeks of quiet doesn't mean the deal is dead. People get busy, priorities shift. I'll send a brief check-in at the three-week mark. Until then, I wait.
pairs.negative: Two weeks without a response is worrying. I've been following up every few days to keep things from falling apart.
